facts.text: Insects make up about 80% of all known animal species on Earth.
The monarch butterfly migrates up to 3,000 miles from Canada to central Mexico each fall.
A honeybee colony can contain up to 60,000 bees during peak summer months.
DEET was developed by the U.S. Army in 1946 to protect soldiers in insect-infested areas.
California's state insect is the California dogface butterfly, designated in 1972.
Mosquitoes are considered the deadliest animal on Earth, killing over 700,000 people annually through disease transmission.
Cockroaches can survive without their heads for up to a week because they breathe through spiracles on their body segments.
Ladybugs are voracious predators that can eat up to 5,000 aphids in their lifetime, making them prized for natural pest control.
The atlas moth of Southeast Asia has a wingspan of up to 12 inches, making it one of the largest moths in the world.
World Bee Day is celebrated on May 20 to raise awareness about the importance of pollinators.
Army ants don't build permanent nests — they form living bridges and bivouacs out of their own bodies.
Spider silk is five times stronger than steel by weight, yet incredibly elastic.
Citronella candles have been used as mosquito repellents since the late 1800s, derived from lemongrass oil.
The praying mantis is one of the few insects that can turn its head 180 degrees to scan its surroundings.
Beetles make up the order Coleoptera, the most species-rich order of insects with over 400,000 known species.
The black widow spider's venom is 15 times stronger than a rattlesnake's, but they inject far less.
Texas designated the monarch butterfly as its state insect in 1995.
Fireflies produce light through a chemical reaction called bioluminescence, using luciferin and luciferase.
Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they cut — they use them to cultivate fungus gardens underground.
Bug zappers, introduced in the 1930s, attract insects with UV light and kill them with an electric grid.
The luna moth has no mouth and lives only about one week as an adult, surviving solely to reproduce.
Entomophagy — the practice of eating insects — is common in over 80% of the world's countries.
National Pollinator Week was designated by the U.S. Senate in 2007 and is celebrated in late June.
The German cockroach is the most common cockroach species found in homes and restaurants worldwide.
Queen bees can lay up to 2,000 eggs per day during peak season.
Jumping spiders have the best vision of any spider and can see in color, with four pairs of eyes.
Permethrin is a synthetic insecticide used to treat clothing and gear for protection against ticks and mosquitoes.
The dung beetle can roll balls of dung 10 times its own weight and navigates using the Milky Way.
New York's state insect is the nine-spotted ladybug, designated in 1989.
Silkworms produce silk fibers used in textiles for over 5,000 years, originating in ancient China.
Mosquitoes are attracted to carbon dioxide, body heat, and certain chemicals in human sweat.
The animated film A Bug's Life was released by Pixar in 1998 and grossed over $363 million worldwide.
Fire ants get their name from the painful, burning sensation caused by their sting.
The golden orb weaver spider creates webs so strong that fishermen in the Pacific Islands use them as nets.
Raid insecticide was first introduced by SC Johnson in 1956 and became a household staple.
Honey never spoils — archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still edible.
The periodical cicadas of Brood X emerge every 17 years across the eastern United States.
Lavender oil is a natural insect repellent effective against mosquitoes, moths, and fleas.
Entomophobia — the fear of insects — is one of the most common specific phobias.
The Goliath beetle of Africa can weigh up to 3.5 ounces, making it the heaviest insect in the world.
Bumblebees can fly at altitudes higher than Mount Everest, according to laboratory simulations.
Pennsylvania's state insect is the Pennsylvania firefly, designated in 1974.
The American cockroach can run at speeds of about 3.4 miles per hour, making it one of the fastest running insects.
A chrysalis is the hard shell a butterfly forms, while a cocoon is the silk casing spun by moths.
Off! insect repellent was introduced by SC Johnson in 1957 as the first consumer DEET product.
Tarantulas are generally harmless to humans, and some species are kept as pets around the world.
An ant can lift 50 times its own body weight, making them proportionally the strongest creatures on Earth.
Japan's national butterfly is the great purple emperor, admired for its iridescent wings.
Mosquito coils, widely used in Asia, release insecticide smoke to repel mosquitoes and have been used since the 1890s.
The praying mantis is the only insect that can turn its head to look over its shoulder.
Lacewings are called 'aphid lions' because their larvae are fierce predators of aphids and other soft-bodied pests.
World Mosquito Day on August 20 marks Sir Ronald Ross's 1897 discovery that mosquitoes transmit malaria.
Monarch butterflies use an internal magnetic compass and the sun to navigate during migration.
The stag beetle's large mandibles are used in male-to-male combat during mating season.
Eucalyptus oil has been used as a natural insect repellent for centuries, particularly effective against mosquitoes.
Georgia's state insect is the honeybee, reflecting the importance of beekeeping to the state's agriculture.
The bombardier beetle defends itself by spraying a boiling chemical mixture from its abdomen at over 200 degrees Fahrenheit.
Colony collapse disorder, first identified around 2006, causes worker bees to abandon their hive.
Dragonflies have been on Earth for over 300 million years, predating dinosaurs by roughly 100 million years.
The movie Antz, released in 1998, featured Woody Allen voicing an ant named Z who questions colony conformity.
Cockroaches can hold their breath underwater for up to 40 minutes.
Butterfly gardens attract pollinators by planting milkweed, coneflowers, zinnias, and lantana.
The waggle dance is a figure-eight movement honeybees perform to communicate the location of food sources.
DDT was banned in the United States in 1972 due to environmental damage, particularly to bird populations.
Ant colonies can contain millions of individuals, with some supercolonies spanning thousands of miles.
The walking stick insect is one of the longest insects in the world, with some species reaching over 24 inches.
Black widow spiders are identified by the distinctive red hourglass marking on their abdomen.
Illinois designated the monarch butterfly as its state insect in 1975.
Pyrethrin, derived from chrysanthemum flowers, has been used as a natural insecticide for over 2,000 years.
Malaria, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes, kills over 600,000 people every year, mostly children in Africa.
The luna moth's pale green wings and long tails make it one of the most recognizable moths in North America.
Carpenter ants don't eat wood — they hollow it out to build their nests, causing structural damage to homes.
Honeybees visit between 50 and 100 flowers during one foraging trip.
Connecticut's state insect is the European praying mantis, designated in 1977.
The flea can jump up to 150 times its own body length, equivalent to a human jumping over a skyscraper.
Dengue fever, transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, affects an estimated 400 million people worldwide each year.
Charles Darwin was an avid beetle collector as a young man, which helped develop his observational skills.
Spider silk has been explored by the military as a potential material for lightweight body armor.
The Asian giant hornet, nicknamed the 'murder hornet,' can grow up to 2 inches long.
Beeswax has been used for thousands of years in candles, cosmetics, and food preservation.
The National Insect of Singapore is the common birdwing butterfly.
Mosquitoes have been on Earth for over 200 million years, surviving multiple mass extinctions.
The hercules beetle, found in Central and South America, can grow up to 7 inches long including its horn.
Ant farms became a popular toy in the 1950s after Milton Levine invented the commercial version in 1956.
Flying cockroaches, common in tropical regions, use their wings primarily to glide from high surfaces.
The painted lady butterfly undertakes a 9,000-mile round trip migration from Africa to the Arctic Circle.
Citronella plants are a popular garden choice because they naturally repel mosquitoes with their scent.
Brood XIX is one of the largest periodical cicada broods, emerging every 13 years across the southeastern United States.
The jewel wasp can zombify a cockroach by injecting venom directly into its brain.
Termites cause an estimated $5 billion in property damage each year in the United States.
Ohio's state insect is the ladybug, chosen through a campaign led by elementary school students in 1975.
The honeybee is the only insect that produces food eaten by humans — honey.
Zika virus, spread by Aedes mosquitoes, caused a global health emergency in 2015-2016.
Karl von Frisch won the Nobel Prize in 1973 for decoding the honeybee waggle dance.
The titan beetle of the Amazon rainforest has mandibles strong enough to snap a pencil in half.
Butterfly wings are covered in thousands of tiny, overlapping scales that create their colorful patterns.
The brown recluse spider is identified by a distinctive violin-shaped marking on its back.
Neem oil is an ancient natural insecticide used in India for thousands of years.
Vermont's state insect is the honeybee, highlighting the state's maple and honey traditions.
Crickets can tell you the temperature — count the chirps in 14 seconds and add 40 for the temperature in Fahrenheit.
The emerald ash borer, an invasive beetle from Asia, has killed hundreds of millions of ash trees in North America since its discovery in 2002.
Beekeeping, also called apiculture, dates back at least 9,000 years, with evidence found in ancient cave paintings.
The cicada is the loudest insect in the world, producing sounds up to 120 decibels.
Mosquito nets treated with insecticide have been one of the most effective tools in reducing malaria deaths.
The monarch butterfly was listed as endangered by the IUCN in July 2022.
Wasps are important predators that help control pest populations in gardens and farms.
The deathwatch beetle gets its name from the ticking sound it makes, once believed to predict death.
Spiders are arachnids, not insects — they have eight legs instead of six and two body segments instead of three.
Maine's state insect is the honeybee, adopted in 1975.
Bed bugs nearly vanished in developed countries by the 1950s due to DDT but made a comeback in the 1990s.
The bullet ant of Central and South America has the most painful sting of any insect, rated 4+ on the Schmidt Pain Index.
Butterflies taste with their feet, using chemoreceptors to identify host plants for egg-laying.
The first synthetic insecticide, DDT, was developed by Swiss chemist Paul Müller, who won the 1948 Nobel Prize for the discovery.
An average beehive produces 60 to 100 pounds of honey per year.
The peacock spider of Australia is known for its vibrant, colorful abdominal flap used in mating displays.
Insect repellent bracelets containing geraniol or citronella are popular but offer limited protection compared to DEET.
Delaware's state insect is the ladybug, designated in 1974 after a campaign by second graders.
The Zika virus outbreak of 2016 led to massive mosquito control spraying programs across the Americas.
Metamorphosis comes in two types: complete (egg, larva, pupa, adult) and incomplete (egg, nymph, adult).
Fireflies synchronize their flashing in places like the Great Smoky Mountains, drawing thousands of visitors each June.
The assassin bug gets its name from its ambush predator tactics, stabbing prey with its sharp beak.
Cockroaches have been around for over 300 million years, making them one of the oldest living insect groups.
The viceroy butterfly mimics the monarch's orange-and-black pattern to deter predators.
Picaridin was introduced as a DEET alternative in the early 2000s and is considered equally effective.
The leafcutter ant colony functions like a factory, with different castes performing specialized roles.
The boll weevil devastated the American cotton industry in the early 1900s, causing billions in crop losses.
New Mexico's state insect is the tarantula hawk wasp, known for its incredibly painful sting.
Dragonflies can fly at speeds up to 35 miles per hour and catch prey mid-flight with nearly 95% success rate.
Bees communicate through pheromones, vibrations, and their famous waggle dance.
The movie The Fly (1986) starred Jeff Goldblum as a scientist who accidentally merges with a housefly during a teleportation experiment.
Ticks are arachnids that transmit Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, and other serious illnesses.
South Carolina's state insect is the Carolina mantis, designated in 1988.
The housefly can taste food with its feet, which are 10 million times more sensitive than the human tongue.
Insecticides containing neonicotinoids have been linked to declining bee populations worldwide.
The giant weta of New Zealand can weigh over 2.5 ounces, heavier than a sparrow.
Monarch butterfly populations overwintering in Mexico dropped by 59% in the 2023-2024 season.
Ant queens can live for decades — some species' queens live over 25 years.
The cuckoo wasp is covered in metallic, jewel-like coloring, earning it the nickname 'jewel wasp.'
Mosquito fish (Gambusia) are stocked in ponds and ditches worldwide specifically to eat mosquito larvae.
Tennessee's state insect is the firefly, designated in 1975, and the state hosts the famous synchronous firefly viewing.
The death's-head hawkmoth features a skull-shaped pattern on its thorax and can squeak when disturbed.
Spider webs come in many forms: orb webs, funnel webs, cobwebs, sheet webs, and trapdoor burrows.
Rachel Carson's book Silent Spring, published in 1962, exposed the dangers of pesticides like DDT.
The beekeeper's smoker calms bees by masking alarm pheromones and triggering a feeding response.
Cockroaches can detect movements as small as 2,000 times the diameter of a hydrogen atom through their cerci.
The monarch butterfly caterpillar feeds exclusively on milkweed, which makes it toxic to predators.
Colorado's state insect is the Colorado hairstreak butterfly, designated in 1996.
The world's smallest insect is the fairyfly wasp, measuring just 0.005 inches long.
Mosquito traps using CO2, heat, and octenol can capture thousands of mosquitoes in a single night.
Termite mounds in Africa and Australia can reach over 17 feet tall and maintain internal temperature regulation.
The trap-jaw ant can snap its mandibles shut at 140 miles per hour, one of the fastest movements in the animal kingdom.
Bee venom therapy, or apitherapy, has been practiced for thousands of years to treat pain and inflammation.
The scarab beetle was sacred in ancient Egypt, symbolizing rebirth and the sun god Ra.
Fruit flies are commonly used in genetics research because they share 75% of disease-causing genes with humans.
Louisiana's state insect is the honeybee, recognizing the state's commercial beekeeping industry.
The brown marmorated stink bug, invasive to North America, was first detected in Pennsylvania in 1998.
Caterpillars have up to 4,000 muscles in their body — compared to about 650 in a human.
Electric bug swatters, shaped like tennis rackets, use a small electric charge to kill flying insects on contact.
The pirate bug is a beneficial insect that feeds on thrips, spider mites, and other small pests.
National Honey Bee Day is celebrated on the third Saturday of August.
The tarantula hawk wasp has a sting so painful it's rated as one of the worst in the insect world.
Japan is home to the world's largest hornet — the Asian giant hornet, which preys on honeybee colonies.
A colony of driver ants in Africa can contain over 20 million individuals and consume everything in their path.
Kansas's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1976.
The woolly bear caterpillar has been used in folklore to predict the severity of winter based on the width of its brown band.
Tsetse flies transmit African sleeping sickness, a parasitic disease that can be fatal if untreated.
The Mexican bean beetle is one of the few ladybug species that is actually a crop pest rather than beneficial.
Bee pollen is collected by foraging bees and sold as a health supplement, though claims are largely unproven.
The gypsy moth, now called the spongy moth, was accidentally introduced to the U.S. in 1869 and causes massive defoliation.
Roach motels, invented in the 1970s, lure cockroaches with bait and trap them on adhesive surfaces.
The glowworm is not actually a worm — it's the larval or adult stage of certain beetle species.
Oregon's state insect is the Oregon swallowtail butterfly, the first insect ever designated as a state symbol in 1979.
Mosquitoes have 47 teeth on their proboscis that help them saw through skin to find blood vessels.
E.O. Wilson, the famous entomologist, was known as the 'ant man' for his groundbreaking research on ant behavior and sociobiology.
The periodical cicada Brood XIII emerges every 17 years in the northern Illinois region.
A single ladybug can eat up to 75 aphids in a day, making it a favorite for organic gardening.
Weaver ants construct nests from leaves stitched together using silk produced by their own larvae.
Insect hotels, made from stacked wood, bamboo, and straw, provide habitat for solitary bees and other beneficial insects.
The bombardier beetle's chemical spray is produced by mixing hydroquinone and hydrogen peroxide in an internal chamber.
Minnesota's state insect is the monarch butterfly, adopted in 2000.
The robber fly is an aerial predator that catches other insects mid-flight, including dragonflies and bees.
Lemon eucalyptus oil is one of the few plant-based repellents recommended by the CDC for mosquito protection.
A bee must visit about 2 million flowers to produce a single pound of honey.
Cockroach allergens are a significant trigger for asthma in urban environments, especially in children.
The violin spider, also known as the brown recluse, is found throughout the south-central United States.
Insect wings evolved only once in evolutionary history, approximately 350 million years ago.
The giant huntsman spider of Laos has a leg span of up to 12 inches, the widest of any spider.
North Carolina's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1973.
Mosquito larvae, called wrigglers, develop in standing water and take about 7-14 days to become adults.
The tomato hornworm is a common garden pest that can defoliate an entire tomato plant in just a few days.
Bee colonies perform thermoregulation — workers fan their wings to cool the hive in summer and cluster together for warmth in winter.
The click beetle can launch itself up to 6 inches in the air with an audible clicking sound when flipped on its back.
Diatomaceous earth is a natural insecticide made from fossilized algae that kills insects by drying out their exoskeletons.
The io moth caterpillar is covered in venomous spines that cause a painful, stinging rash on contact.
Florida's state butterfly is the zebra longwing, known for its slow, graceful flight and long lifespan.
The ironclad beetle can withstand forces of up to 39,000 times its body weight without being crushed.
Parasitic wasps lay their eggs inside other insects, and the larvae consume the host from the inside.
Bed bug infestations surged in major cities worldwide during the 2010s and 2020s, linked to increased travel.
Ants leave pheromone trails for other ants to follow, creating efficient foraging highways.
The Africanized honeybee, or 'killer bee,' is a hybrid that is more aggressive than the European honeybee.
Maryland's state insect is the Baltimore checkerspot butterfly, designated in 1973.
The giant water bug, also known as the 'toe biter,' can deliver one of the most painful bites of any insect.
Boric acid has been used since the early 1900s as an effective cockroach killer in homes.
The oleander hawk moth caterpillar has large eyespots that make it look like a small snake to deter predators.
Moths outnumber butterflies by about 10 to 1, with approximately 160,000 known moth species.
Insect farming for protein is one of the fastest-growing agricultural sectors, projected to be a $8 billion industry by 2030.
The doodlebug is the larval stage of the antlion, which digs cone-shaped sand traps to catch ants.
Honeybees are not native to North America — they were brought by European colonists in the 1600s.
The wheel bug, a type of assassin bug, has a distinctive gear-shaped crest on its thorax.
Wisconsin's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1977.
The National Pest Management Association estimates that termites cause $5 billion in damage annually in the U.S.
Yellow fever, transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, killed thousands during the construction of the Panama Canal.
Spider mites, though not true insects, are among the most destructive pests for houseplants and crops.
The rose chafer beetle is a common garden pest that feeds on roses, grapes, and other flowering plants.
Earth Day, celebrated on April 22, often highlights the critical role insects play in ecosystems.
The mayfly has the shortest adult lifespan of any insect — some species live for less than 24 hours.
Fleas can transmit plague, typhus, and tapeworms, and were responsible for spreading the Black Death in medieval Europe.
The two-spotted spider mite can reproduce so quickly that a single female can produce a million descendants in a month.
Alaska has no official state insect, as insect diversity is relatively low in its cold climate.
Insecticidal soap works by dissolving the waxy coating on soft-bodied insects, causing them to dehydrate.
The green lacewing lays its eggs on slender stalks to protect them from predators, including its own siblings.
Arizona's state insect is not yet designated, though the two-tailed swallowtail butterfly has been proposed.
Honeybees perform a 'piping' sound by vibrating their flight muscles, which signals swarming.
The dobsonfly male has enormous mandibles that look fearsome but are too weak to bite effectively.
Approximately 2 billion people worldwide regularly eat insects as part of their diet.
The trapdoor spider builds a camouflaged burrow with a hinged door made of soil, vegetation, and silk.
Mississippi's state insect is the honeybee, with many residents participating in small-scale beekeeping.
Moth balls contain naphthalene or paradichlorobenzene and have been used to repel clothes moths since the 19th century.
The monarch butterfly's migration was only fully mapped in the 1970s when researcher Fred Urquhart found their wintering grounds in Mexico.
Carpenter bees drill perfectly round holes in wood to create nesting tunnels for their eggs.
The treehoppers are among the most bizarre-looking insects, with elaborate helmet-like structures on their thorax.
Tick populations have expanded significantly due to warmer winters caused by climate change.
The European corn borer, first detected in the U.S. in 1917, causes over $1 billion in crop losses annually.
Nebraska's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1975.
The whirligig beetle spins in circles on the water surface and has divided eyes to see above and below water simultaneously.
Mosquitoes prefer to bite people with Type O blood, according to several research studies.
The giant silk moth family includes the cecropia, polyphemus, and luna moths, all native to North America.
Natural predators like bats, birds, frogs, and dragonflies are essential for keeping mosquito populations in check.
The first insect to be sent into space was a fruit fly, launched on a V-2 rocket in 1947.
Velvet ants are actually wingless wasps whose sting is so painful they're nicknamed 'cow killers.'
Oklahoma's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1992.
The spittlebug nymph creates a frothy mass called 'cuckoo spit' to hide from predators and stay moist.
Insect-based dog and cat food products have emerged as sustainable alternatives to traditional pet food.
The convergent ladybug gathers in massive aggregations in the mountains of California during winter.
Citronella torches are a popular outdoor solution for repelling mosquitoes during summer gatherings.
The rhinoceros beetle can carry 850 times its own weight, making it the strongest animal relative to its size.
Head lice have co-evolved with humans for at least 100,000 years and are species-specific.
The cabbage white butterfly is one of the most widespread butterflies in the world and a common garden pest.
Integrated pest management (IPM) combines biological, cultural, and chemical methods to control insects sustainably.
Utah's state insect is the honeybee, reflecting the state's nickname as 'The Beehive State.'
The peppered moth became famous as an example of natural selection during the Industrial Revolution when darker moths thrived in polluted areas.
Mosquito dunks containing Bti bacteria are dropped into standing water to kill larvae without harming other wildlife.
The hissing cockroach of Madagascar produces a loud hissing sound by forcing air through its spiracles.
Migrating painted lady butterflies have been tracked by radar crossing the Sahara Desert.
The Chinese mantis was introduced to the U.S. in 1896 as a pest control agent and is now common across the country.
Georgia's state butterfly is the eastern tiger swallowtail, separate from its state insect designation.
The kissing bug transmits Chagas disease through its feces while biting sleeping humans, affecting millions in Latin America.
Electric insect traps with UV light are most effective against flies and moths but are largely ineffective against mosquitoes.
Aphids can reproduce without mating through parthenogenesis, and females can be born already pregnant.
The world's longest insect is Chan's megastick from Borneo, measuring over 22 inches.
The love bug, common in the Gulf states, swarms in massive numbers during spring and fall mating seasons.
Indiana's state insect is the Say's firefly, named after Thomas Say, the father of American entomology.
Leaf insects are masters of camouflage, with bodies that look exactly like green or brown leaves.
Ultrasonic pest repellers claim to repel insects with high-frequency sound, but scientific evidence of their effectiveness is limited.
The Hessian fly was blamed for crop failures during the American Revolution and was supposedly brought by Hessian troops.
Beeswax wraps have gained popularity as an eco-friendly alternative to plastic wrap for food storage.
The diving beetle is an aquatic predator that carries an air bubble under its wing covers to breathe underwater.
West Nile virus was first detected in the United States in 1999 in New York City, spread by Culex mosquitoes.
The hummingbird hawk-moth hovers and feeds from flowers just like a hummingbird, often fooling observers.
Virginia's state insect is the eastern tiger swallowtail butterfly, designated in 1991.
Australia's funnel-web spider is one of the most venomous spiders in the world, with bites that can be fatal to humans.
The periodical cicada emergence of 2024 saw both Brood XIII and Brood XIX emerge simultaneously for the first time in 221 years.
Insect decline has been called the 'insect apocalypse,' with some studies showing 40% of species at risk of extinction.
The blister beetle produces cantharidin, a toxic chemical that causes painful blisters on human skin.
Ant bridges, formed by fire ants linking their bodies together, allow colonies to cross water during floods.
Hawaii's state insect is the Kamehameha butterfly, one of only two butterfly species native to the islands.
Foggers, or 'bug bombs,' release insecticide aerosol throughout a room but are considered less effective than targeted treatments.
The thorn bug has a large, horn-like protrusion on its thorax that makes it look like a plant thorn.
Bees can recognize human faces, distinguishing individual faces in experimental settings.
The greenhouse whitefly is a major pest of tomatoes, cucumbers, and ornamental plants grown indoors.
Monarch Watch, founded in 1992, coordinates citizen science efforts to tag and track monarch butterflies.
The webspinner insect creates silk galleries on tree bark using glands on its front legs.
Peppermint oil is used as a natural repellent for spiders, ants, and mosquitoes in many households.
Massachusetts's state insect is the ladybug, designated in 1974.
The water strider walks on water using hydrophobic hairs on its legs that don't break the surface tension.
Earwigs get their name from an old superstition that they crawled into people's ears, which is largely a myth.
The cotton boll weevil monument in Enterprise, Alabama, honors the pest that forced farmers to diversify crops.
Parasitoid wasps are used commercially in greenhouses to control whitefly, aphid, and caterpillar populations.
Cockroach milk, produced by the Pacific beetle cockroach, is being researched as a potential superfood.
The flower mantis disguises itself as a flower to ambush pollinating insects that land on it.
Mosquito nets save an estimated 68,000 lives per year in sub-Saharan Africa.
The tiger moth produces ultrasonic clicks to jam bat sonar, effectively making itself invisible to its predator.
New Hampshire's state insect is the ladybug, designated in 1977.
The puss caterpillar, also called 'asp,' is the most venomous caterpillar in North America.
Insect identification apps like iNaturalist and Picture Insect have made citizen entomology widely accessible.
The red imported fire ant, introduced to the U.S. in the 1930s, now infests over 300 million acres across the South.
Black soldier fly larvae are increasingly used to convert food waste into animal feed and fertilizer.
The glasswing butterfly has transparent wings that make it nearly invisible in flight.
Bee stings cause anaphylaxis in about 3% of adults who are stung, making it a medical emergency.
The multicolored Asian lady beetle was introduced to the U.S. for pest control but has become a nuisance itself.
Missouri's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1985.
Insect photography, or macro photography, has become a popular hobby for capturing the hidden beauty of bugs.
The antlion adult looks like a delicate dragonfly and is sometimes called a lacewing, though they are different families.
DEET remains the gold standard for insect repellents, providing protection for up to 12 hours when applied properly.
The darkling beetle is one of the most common stored-product pests, infesting grain and flour.
Honeybee venom contains over 60 different compounds, including melittin, which has anti-inflammatory properties.
The Carolina wolf spider is South Carolina's state spider, the only state to designate an official state spider.
The earwig's pincers, called cerci, are used for defense, catching prey, and folding wings.
The 17-year cicadas that emerged in 2021 were part of Brood X, one of the largest broods in the eastern U.S.
Mealworms, the larvae of darkling beetles, are one of the most commonly farmed edible insects.
The sawfly larva resembles a caterpillar but is actually related to bees, wasps, and ants.
Insect repellent clothing treated with permethrin can retain its effectiveness through 70 washes.
The orchid mantis of Southeast Asia mimics a flower so perfectly that pollinators land directly on it.
Kentucky's state insect is the viceroy butterfly, designated in 1990.
The elm bark beetle spreads Dutch elm disease, which has killed millions of elm trees across North America and Europe.
Paper wasps build open-celled nests from chewed wood pulp that resemble upside-down umbrellas.
Mosquito-borne diseases collectively kill more people than any other animal-related cause.
The Indian stick insect is one of the most popular pet insects, known for being easy to care for.
The glow of a firefly is produced at nearly 100% efficiency with almost no heat — called 'cold light.'
Beneficial nematodes are microscopic worms used to control grubs, fleas, and other soil-dwelling insect pests.
The io moth's large eyespots startle predators when the moth suddenly spreads its hindwings.
Montana's state insect is the mourning cloak butterfly, designated in 2001.
Spider silk has been studied for potential use in surgical sutures, artificial ligaments, and parachute cords.
The kissing bug is named because it often bites humans around the mouth and eyes while they sleep.
The first International Day for Biological Diversity highlighted insect biodiversity loss on May 22, 2000.
A queen termite can live for 25 to 50 years, the longest lifespan of any insect.
The boxelder bug is a common fall nuisance pest that congregates on the sunny sides of buildings.
Insect frass — the excrement of herbivorous insects — is used as a nutrient-rich organic fertilizer.
The dragonfly nymph lives underwater for up to five years before emerging as a flying adult.
Wyoming does not have a designated state insect, though Sheridan's green stonefly has been suggested.
The cecropia moth is the largest native moth in North America, with a wingspan up to 7 inches.
Traditional Japanese art includes detailed depictions of insects, reflecting cultural appreciation of nature and seasons.
The lanternfly, or spotted lanternfly, is an invasive pest from Asia that threatens grape, fruit, and hardwood industries in the U.S.
Mosquitoes need only a bottle cap's worth of standing water to breed successfully.
The Christmas beetle is an iconic insect in Australia, appearing in large numbers during the holiday season.
Mantis shrimp are not insects but are known for the fastest punch in the animal kingdom.
Michigan's state insect is not officially designated, though the green darner dragonfly has been proposed.
Bees produce royal jelly to feed larvae, with queen larvae receiving an exclusive royal jelly diet that triggers their development.
The mud dauber wasp builds tube-like nests from mud and is generally non-aggressive toward humans.
Ticks can be effectively removed with fine-tipped tweezers by pulling steadily upward without twisting.
The desert locust can form swarms covering hundreds of square miles, consuming their weight in food daily.
Forensic entomology uses insect evidence — especially blow fly larval development — to estimate time of death.
Nevada does not have a state insect but has the vivid dancer damselfly as its state invertebrate.
The tomato hornworm covered in white cocoons is being parasitized by braconid wasps — leave it alone to help the wasps.
National Moth Week, held in late July, encourages people to observe and document moths.
The death-feigning beetle plays dead when threatened, going completely limp and motionless.
Honey contains natural antibacterial properties due to its low water content, acidity, and hydrogen peroxide production.
The water scorpion is not a true scorpion but an aquatic insect with raptorial front legs and a breathing siphon.
The Xerces Society, founded in 1971, is a major conservation organization dedicated to protecting invertebrates, especially pollinators.
Swarming termites emerge in spring, often mistaken for flying ants, and indicate a nearby colony.
The green bottle fly is one of the first insects to arrive at decaying organic matter, making it important in forensics.
Cricket flour is made from ground crickets and contains more protein per gram than beef.
The Madagascar sunset moth is considered one of the most beautiful insects in the world, with iridescent rainbow-colored wings.
Biological pest control using ladybugs dates back to 1888, when they were imported to control cottony cushion scale in California.
The stink bug releases a foul-smelling chemical from glands on its thorax as a defense mechanism.
Honeybees flap their wings about 200 times per second, producing their distinctive buzzing sound.
The goliath birdeater tarantula of South America is the world's largest spider by mass, weighing up to 6.2 ounces.
Chiggers are tiny mite larvae that cause intensely itchy welts by injecting digestive enzymes into skin.
The damselfly looks like a slender dragonfly but holds its wings together above its body when resting.
Rosy maple moths, with their pink and yellow coloring, are among the most colorful moths in North America.
The Aedes mosquito that spreads dengue and Zika bites primarily during the day, unlike most mosquito species.
South Dakota's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 1978.
The sphinx moth, also called a hawk moth, can hover in place like a hummingbird while feeding on nectar.
Insects have an open circulatory system — their blood, called hemolymph, flows freely through the body cavity.
The June bug is actually a scarab beetle that emerges in late spring and is attracted to porch lights.
Backyard beekeeping has surged in popularity, with the number of U.S. hobbyist beekeepers tripling since 2008.
The spider wasp hunts spiders by paralyzing them with a sting and dragging them into a burrow for its larvae.
Grasshoppers can jump up to 20 times their body length — equivalent to a human jumping the length of a basketball court.
Permethrin-treated military uniforms have been standard issue for U.S. troops since the 1990s to prevent insect-borne diseases.
The milkweed bug feeds on milkweed seeds and, like the monarch, uses the plant's toxins for defense.
Solitary bees, including mason and leafcutter bees, are often more efficient pollinators than honeybees.
West Virginia's state insect is the honeybee, designated in 2002.
The hover fly mimics bees and wasps with yellow-and-black stripes but is completely harmless and a great pollinator.
Insect antennae serve as sensory organs for smell, taste, touch, and even hearing.
The fall armyworm is a devastating crop pest in Africa and Asia that can destroy a cornfield in days.
The polyphemus moth has large eyespots on its hindwings named after the cyclops of Greek mythology.
Bees can be trained to detect explosives and drugs using their extraordinarily sensitive sense of smell.
Ant lions got their name because they primarily prey on ants that fall into their sand pit traps.
The Asian lady beetle, often confused with native ladybugs, releases a yellow fluid with an unpleasant odor when disturbed.
Silverfish are wingless insects that have remained virtually unchanged for over 400 million years.
The botfly lays its eggs on mosquitoes, which then transfer the larvae to mammals — including humans — when they bite.
Insect exoskeletons are made of chitin, the same material found in crab and shrimp shells.
The world's first insect emoji, the honeybee, was added to Unicode in 2010.
National Save a Spider Day is celebrated on March 14 to raise awareness about spider conservation.
